sections.heading: DoDEA Pacific Schools in Okinawa
sections.body: Department of Defense Education Activity (DoDEA) operates schools on base for military-connected students K-12. These are fully accredited US schools — same curriculum, sports, and activities as stateside.

contacts.label: DoDEA Pacific - Okinawa District
contacts.note: Main DoDEA district office for Okinawa. Enrollment and general questions.

contacts.label: DoDEA Okinawa Website
contacts.note: School listings, calendars, enrollment forms, and contacts.

steps: Contact the DoDEA district office or visit dodea.edu to find your assigned school based on your housing area.
Gather enrollment documents: birth certificate, immunization records, previous school records, and sponsor's orders.
Submit enrollment paperwork at the school office — walk-ins are accepted but call ahead.
Your child will be placed based on grade level and assessed if transferring mid-year.
School buses run from most housing areas — confirm routes with the school.
tips: Enroll as soon as possible — some programs (special education, gifted) have waitlists.
DoDEA transcripts transfer seamlessly back to US schools when you PCS.
School liaisons on base can help with the enrollment process — contact your installation's School Liaison Officer (SLO).
DoDEA follows a similar academic calendar to US schools with summers off.

sections.heading: DoDEA Schools by Area
sections.body: Schools are assigned based on your housing area. Contact the district office to confirm your assigned school.

contacts.label: Okinawa Christian Academy (OCA)
contacts.note: Private Christian school. Accepts SOFA-status families. Grades K-12.

sections.heading: School Liaison Officers (SLO)
sections.body: Every installation has a School Liaison Officer whose sole job is to help military families navigate the school system. They are one of the most underutilized resources on base — use them.
steps: Contact your installation's SLO as soon as you arrive.
The SLO can help with enrollment, school selection, and special education transitions.
If your child has an IEP or 504 plan, bring copies — the SLO will help transfer services.
SLOs can advocate on your behalf if you encounter issues with enrollment or placement.
tips: SLO services are free and available to all military families.
If your child has special needs, contact the SLO before you PCS — early coordination is critical.
SLOs can also help with college prep and transcript requests for high schoolers.

sections.heading: Special Education & IEP Services
sections.body: DoDEA provides special education services including IEP and 504 plans. Services transfer from stateside schools but re-evaluation may be required. Start this process early.

contacts.label: DoDEA Special Education
contacts.note: Contact the district office to begin IEP transfer and evaluation process.

contacts.label: Exceptional Family Member Program (EFMP)
contacts.note: EFMP coordinates support for families with special needs members. Enrollment is mandatory for EFMP families.

steps: Bring all current IEP or 504 documents with you — do not rely on records being transferred.
Contact the DoDEA district office before you arrive to begin the transition process.
An IEP meeting will be scheduled within 30 days of enrollment.
Services may look different than stateside — discuss options with the IEP team.
EFMP enrollment is mandatory and ensures support services follow your family.
tips: The EFMP coordinator can be a powerful advocate — build that relationship early.
Some specialized therapies may not be available on base — ask about off-base referrals.
Naval Hospital Okinawa has pediatric therapy services for eligible dependents.
Document everything in writing — email confirmations of all IEP discussions.

sections.heading: Homeschooling in Okinawa
sections.body: Homeschooling is permitted for SOFA-status families in Okinawa. You follow your home state's homeschool laws, not Japanese law. An active homeschool community exists on island.

contacts.label: Okinawa Homeschool Association
contacts.note: Search Facebook for active Okinawa homeschool groups — very active community.

contacts.label: Camp Foster School Liaison
contacts.note: Can advise on base facility access for homeschool families.

steps: Confirm your home state's homeschool requirements — you are subject to your state's laws.
Register with your home state if required.
Connect with the Okinawa homeschool community on Facebook — co-ops and group classes are common.
Homeschool families may have access to some DoDEA extracurricular activities — confirm with the SLO.
Use base library resources — Kadena and Foster libraries have extensive homeschool sections.
tips: The Okinawa homeschool community is tight-knit and welcoming to new families.
Co-op classes covering science labs, PE, and electives are widely available.
Base facilities like the gym, pool, and library are generally accessible to homeschool families.
Document your curriculum and keep records — you'll need them when you PCS back stateside.

sections.heading: Enrolling in Japanese Local Schools
sections.body: Some SOFA families choose to enroll their children in Japanese public or private schools for the cultural and language experience. This is permitted but requires coordination with your village office.

contacts.label: Your Village Municipal Office
contacts.note: Contact your village's education department to begin enrollment. Bring your residence card.

steps: Contact your village municipal office (役場) education department.
Bring your residence card, passport, and sponsor's orders.
Your child will be assigned to the local school based on your address.
Japanese schools do not provide English instruction — prepare your child for full immersion.
School year runs April to March in Japan — mid-year enrollment is possible but disruptive.
tips: Japanese schools require uniforms — budget for this upfront.
The experience can be incredibly valuable for children — many families consider it a highlight of their tour.
Connect with other SOFA families who have done this — the Facebook community has many who can advise.
Lunch (給食) is provided at Japanese public schools and is included in fees.
The Japanese school year starts in April — if possible, time your enrollment to the start of a semester.
